You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

Conversation started: Sunday, April 12, 2026 01:08 AM
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
Provider: openrouter

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Please do not use markdown as it does not render. You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md]
# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/CLAUDE.md]
# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

   127|### Key Patterns Identified from New Cases
   128|- **Premises liability cases are litigation-bound**: PL cases against commercial property owners with dedicated defense counsel are much more likely to proceed to full litigation than auto accident cases

   515|### Key New Patterns Identified
   516|- **Inter-counsel distribution disputes as litigation trigger**: When policy limits are tendered but must be split among multiple plaintiffs' counsel, failure to agree on allocation forces litigation — a novel trigger distinct from low offers, coverage denial, or SOL pressure
   517|- **EvenUP demand tool integration**: Third-party AI tool used to generate demand package, which was then edited and sent — fastest demand-to-offer turnaround observed (1 day)
   518|- **Estate cases create a distinct family-contact dynamic**: When the client has died, the surviving spouse is both the legal party (estate representative) and the emotional contact, adding complexity beyond the standard family-proxy pattern

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Companion Case Management
    18|
    19|> When multiple family members are injured in the same accident, each client requires a separate case file but the cases must be coordinated. Companion cases share the same defendant, insurance carriers, and litigation timeline, but each plaintiff has independent medical treatment, damages, and settlement negotiations. The primary client (typically the parent/driver) often serves as the coordinator for all cases.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|1. **Intake**: One family member signs up first; firm discovers additional family members during the welcome call or initial contact
    24|2. **File Creation**: Each companion gets a separate case file with separate intake documents
    25|3. **Coordination**: Parent/primary client is expected to facilitate the companion's document signing, provide their information (SSN, medical providers, insurance)
    26|4. **Records Collection**: Each companion's medical providers must be identified and records requested independently — this often falls on the primary client to facilitate
    27|5. **Litigation**: Companions may be joined in one lawsuit (e.g., "Plaintiff A et al. vs. Defendant") or filed separately. Depositions are scheduled sequentially for companions
    28|6. **Settlement**: Each companion's settlement is evaluated independently, but mediation may address all cases simultaneously
    29|
    30|## Evidence
    31|
    32|- In Case G (MVA), the client's daughter was identified as a companion case during the welcome call. Her Filevine file had not been created yet, requiring coordination. The primary client was asked repeatedly over 6+ months to obtain Children's Hospital records for the companion but failed to do so. Both cases were joined in one lawsuit (Boone 24-CI-00452). During mediation via Zoom, both cases received separate LBF (last best & final) offers. Mediation failed for both.
    33|- In Case I (MVA), a companion witness (Dana Jackson) was deposed alongside the client. Depositions were scheduled sequentially via Zoom — the client was told "we are going to do Dana's deposition first so just chill and I will shoot you a text when we are about done." Coordinating two depositions on the same day required active text communication to manage timing.
    34|- In an MVA case (multi-plaintiff, Floyd County IN), three plaintiffs from the same accident were joined in one lawsuit. The complaint was filed as a multi-plaintiff action. Deposition scheduling required coordination across all plaintiffs through court reporting services. Mediation was being scheduled for all cases simultaneously. The case crossed state lines (KY firm filing in IN), adding jurisdictional complexity for companion case coordination. Two of the settled BI claims were resolved separately per plaintiff, confirming that settlement amounts are independent even in joint litigation.
    35|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), three additional passengers in the same vehicle were each represented by different law firms (4 total plaintiffs). The at-fault driver's insurance tendered policy limits (25/50) to be divided among all four plaintiffs. The coordination challenge shifted from records collection to inter-counsel distribution: the firm had to email all three other firms to negotiate allocation. This process stalled for months (Jan-Apr 2024+) as counsel for one co-plaintiff was slow to provide medical bills needed for allocation. The delay frustrated the client, who called repeatedly asking for updates and was told "we are still waiting on a reply on the one person we're waiting for medical bills." When distribution could not be agreed upon, the case moved to litigation with all plaintiffs joined in one lawsuit. Depositions for multiple plaintiffs in the joint suit were coordinated through defense counsel.
    36|
    37|## Agent Guidance
    38|
    39|1. During intake, ask whether other family members were in the vehicle and injured
    40|2. Create separate case files for each companion immediately — do not delay
    41|3. Send intake documents to companions as early as possible
    42|4. Track records collection for each companion independently
    43|5. When the primary client is the gateway to companion information, set explicit deadlines and follow up
    44|6. For litigation, coordinate with the attorney on whether to file jointly or separately
    45|7. Schedule depositions for companions on the same day when possible to minimize coordination burden
    46|8. During mediation, prepare separate settlement evaluations for each companion
    47|
    48|## Pitfalls
    49|
    50|- Discovering companion cases days/weeks after the primary intake (records requests are delayed)
    51|", "total_lines": 61, "file_size": 6321, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=51 to continue reading (showing 1-50 of 61 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Unreachable Client Patterns
    19|
    20|> Some PI clients are persistently difficult to reach — their voicemail is full, they don't return calls, and emails go unanswered. This pattern is distinct from client frustration (where the client is actively trying to reach the firm). Unreachable clients create a bottleneck because the firm needs their input to proceed with intake, treatment confirmation, and case management. Persistent multi-channel contact attempts are required, sometimes over weeks.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Common Unreachability Signals
    25|1. **Voicemail full**: The client's voicemail box is full, preventing the firm from leaving a message
    26|2. **No answer, no callback**: Multiple outbound calls with no answer and no return call
    27|3. **Phone number changes**: Client changes phone numbers without notifying the firm
    28|4. **Email unresponsive**: Welcome emails and information requests go unanswered
    29|
    30|### Multi-Channel Escalation
    31|The firm follows a predictable escalation pattern when a client is unreachable:
    32|1. **Day 1**: Outbound call → no answer / voicemail full
    33|2. **Day 1**: Text message sent as fallback
    34|3. **Day 2-3**: Second outbound call attempt + email
    35|4. **Week 2**: Follow-up task created ("Two Week follow up with client")
    36|5. **Week 3-4**: Additional call attempts, emails, texts
    37|6. **Ongoing**: "Still unreachable" logged in case notes
    38|7. **Eventually**: Client responds via a different channel or new phone number
    39|
    40|### Impact on Case Timeline
    41|- Intake tasks cannot be completed without client input (providers list, insurance card, SSN)
    42|- File Setup stalls — the case sits in File Setup for months instead of progressing
    43|- Medical records requests are delayed because providers aren't confirmed
    44|- The case may ultimately be declined partly due to the difficulty of working it without client cooperation
    45|
    46|### Client Eventually Resurfaces
    47|Unreachable clients often do eventually make contact, sometimes with a new phone number. When they do:
    48|- They expect the case to be progressing despite their absence
    49|- They may provide the requested information belatedly
    50|- The firm must quickly re-engage and catch up on delayed tasks
    51|", "total_lines": 91, "file_size": 8443, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=51 to continue reading (showing 1-50 of 91 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    15|  - negotiation-after-demand
    16|---
    17|
    18|# Demand Preparation Workflow
    19|
    20|> The demand phase begins when the client's treatment is complete and all medical records/bills have been collected. The transition to "Demand in Progress" is a formal phase change that triggers a series of tasks: final records requests, records review for completeness and relatedness, lien identification, and ultimately the drafting and sending of the demand package to the insurance adjuster.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Prerequisites for Demand Phase Entry
    25|1. Client confirms treatment is complete (via phone call, documented in log)
    26|2. All known medical providers have been identified
    27|3. Outstanding records/bills requests have been sent
    28|4. Attorney or case manager authorizes the phase change
    29|
    30|### Demand Preparation Sequence
    31|1. **Phase Change**: Status changed from "Treatment" to "Demand in Progress" with reason logged
    32|2. **Final Records Requests**: Any outstanding records/bills are requested or re-requested
    33|3. **Records Review**: Medical records are reviewed for:
    34|   - Relatedness to the accident (unrelated treatment must be separated)
    35|   - Completeness (all visits documented)
    36|   - Referrals to other providers discovered in records (new providers must be added)
    37|4. **Lien Identification**: Health insurance liens identified and lien amounts requested (e.g., from Rawlings/WellCare)
    38|5. **Demand Drafting**: Attorney prepares the demand package
    39|6. **Demand Sent**: Package sent to the BI adjuster; follow-up task created
    40|
    41|### Records Review Reveals New Providers
    42|A critical pattern: when records from one provider are received and reviewed, they may reference visits to other providers not previously on file. These must be added and their records requested before the demand can proceed.
    43|
    44|### Adjuster Proactive Contact During Demand Prep
    45|Insurance adjusters may proactively call to check on claim status during the demand preparation period, especially as the case approaches SOL. This should be documented and the adjuster given a general update without committing to specific timelines.
    46|
    47|## Evidence
    48|
    49|- In an auto accident case (DOI 2/2023), the client confirmed treatment was complete during a phone call on 3/11/2024 ("done treating with all medical providers listed on file. No more hospital visits."). Phase was changed to "Demand in Progress" that same day. Records review revealed a referral to White House Clinics and King's Eye Care not previously on file — both had to be added and records requested. Lien request was sent to The Rawlings Company (WellCare) during this phase. The BI adjuster (Clearcover) proactively called to follow up on claim status.
    50|- In the same case, records from providers were separated for relatedness (unrelated visits excluded) before demand preparation.
    51|", "total_lines": 84, "file_size": 7079, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=51 to continue reading (showing 1-50 of 84 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Premises Liability Case Patterns
    19|
    20|> Premises liability (PL) cases — typically slip-and-fall incidents at commercial properties — follow a different trajectory than auto accident cases. They are more likely to proceed to litigation, involve property owner defense counsel early, and require more extensive expert work (FCE, IME, vocational/economic experts). The liability analysis focuses on property owner notice and maintenance rather than traffic fault.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|PL cases differ from auto accident cases in several key ways:
    25|
    26|### Liability Analysis
    27|- **Property owner notice**: Was the hazardous condition known to the owner? How long did it exist?
    28|- **Maintenance records**: Were inspection and maintenance protocols followed?
    29|- **Incident reports**: Was an incident report filed at the time of the fall?
    30|- **Surveillance footage**: Commercial properties may have video of the incident; preservation requests should be sent immediately
    31|- **Third-party defendants**: Banks, stores, and other commercial entities typically have dedicated defense counsel (not insurance adjusters)
    32|
    33|### Litigation Tendency
    34|- PL cases are more likely to proceed to litigation than auto accident cases
    35|- Defense counsel for commercial property owners may aggressively litigate through discovery, depositions, and motions practice
    36|- Mediation is commonly attempted before trial
    37|
    38|### Expert Work
    39|- **Functional Capacity Evaluations (FCE)**: Used to document physical limitations from the injury
    40|- **Independent Medical Examinations (IME)**: May be requested by either side; amendments may be needed as treatment progresses
    41|- **Economist/Vocational Reports**: When the injury affects earning capacity, vocational economics experts are retained
    42|
    43|### Timeline
    44|- PL cases tend to run significantly longer than auto accident cases (3-6+ years observed vs. 1-2 years for typical MVA)
    45|- Discovery deadlines, motions practice, and mediation scheduling extend the timeline
    46|
    47|## Evidence
    48|
    49|- In a premises liability case (slip-and-fall at a commercial bank, 2019 incident), the case progressed through litigation with complaint filing, interrogatories (IROG), discovery, depositions, mediation scheduling, IME, FCE, and economist report requests — spanning 2019-2025+. Defense counsel for the property owner actively participated in discovery and motions practice. Mediation was scheduled then cancelled. The case required certified medical records for litigation (not just demand copies).
    50|- The same case involved multiple medical providers across orthopedics, neurosurgery, PCP, and imaging facilities, with the attorney directing additional imaging (MRI) based on IME findings.
    51|- In Case T (premises liability, slip-and-fall, DOI 9/2023), the case involved a commercial property where the third-party claims adjuster (Soundview Claims Solutions) and defense counsel (Reminger Co. LPA) managed the defense. The claim was handled through a third-party claims administrator rather than a traditional insurer — confirming that PL cases against commercial properties often route through specialized claims management firms. The defense counsel engaged in direct email correspondence with the plaintiff attorney regarding settlement, with both sides CC'ing the client. The claim was in active negotiation phase with the defense attorney responding to settlement communications over several months (9/2025 to 2/2026). The case confirms that PL cases with defense counsel involvement take an adversarial posture from early in the process, with defense firms (in this case a national defense firm) handling correspondence. The health insurance lien (The Phia Group, $1,926.79) had to be tracked during negotiation. Treatment spanned orthopedics, hospital, imaging, and DME — typical of a fall injury case.
    52|- Tax documentation was required for economic damages claims, and the defense counsel specifically challenged tax document accuracy (wrong year provided).
    53|- In a third PL case (slip-and-fall at a national retail chain, DOI 2/2023, KY jurisdiction), the client sustained injuries requiring treatment across 10+ medical providers (orthopedics, physical therapy, pain management, home medical equipment, radiology) with total billings exceeding $137K. The claim was managed by a third-party claims administrator (not the retailer's own insurer). Complaint was filed naming three defendants: the retail chain, the property management company, and the property owner entity. Active litigation included mediation (unsuccessful) and ongoing defense counsel correspondence. Health insurance lien exceeded $35K. Records collection extended 2+ years post-incident, with the firm still calling providers for outstanding records and bills during active litigation. The case demonstrates that large-retailer PL cases involve multi-layered corporate defendants with separate legal representation.
    54|- In a second PL case (fall at commercial property, DOI 9/2023), the case was transferred from prior counsel with SOL approaching within 60 days. The firm onboarded and moved to litigation (complaint filing + discovery requests) the same day as file setup — skipping demand entirely. Multiple defendants and their employer/business entities were named, each represented by separate defense counsel. The case involved 15+ medical providers, insurance carrier service via out-of-state sheriff, and multi-law-firm defense representation. The client exhibited a pattern of frequent missed calls (20+ over 7 months) with periodic attorney return calls.
    55|
    56|## Agent Guidance
    57|
    58|1. At intake for PL cases, immediately send a preservation letter to the property owner requesting incident reports, maintenance logs, and surveillance footage
    59|2. Identify the property owner's insurance carrier AND their defense counsel — PL cases often have dedicated law firms representing the property owner
    60|3. Plan for litigation from the start: request certified records (not just copies) from medical providers
    61|4. Budget for expert expenses early: FCE ($500-600+), IME, vocational/economic reports
    62|5. Track discovery deadlines meticulously — interrogatories, document production, deposition scheduling
    63|6. Coordinate mediation scheduling between all parties (mediator, defense counsel, client) well in advance
    64|7. When the attorney directs additional medical evaluation based on IME/FCE findings, prioritize scheduling
    65|
    66|## Pitfalls
    67|
    68|- Treating a PL case like an auto accident case (PL is more complex and litigation-heavy)
    69|- Failing to request surveillance footage promptly — it may be overwritten
    70|- Not requesting certified records for litigation (regular copies may be insufficient for court)
    71|- Providing incorrect documents to defense counsel (e.g., wrong tax year) — verify all documents before production
    72|- Not coordinating expert evaluations (FCE, IME) early enough — scheduling can take weeks/months
    73|- Allowing mediation to be cancelled without promptly rescheduling
    74|
    75|## Related
    76|
    77|- [[litigation-process-patterns]] — PL cases frequently enter litigation
    78|- [[medical-records-collection]] — PL cases require certified records for litigation
    79|- [[expert-reports-and-evaluations]] — FCE, IME, and vocational reports are common in PL
    80|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — PL cases may skip demand phase and go directly to litigation
    81|", "total_lines": 80, "file_size": 7767, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

Multiple defendants and their employer/business entities were named, each represented by separate defense counsel. The case involved 15+ medical providers, insurance carrier service via out-of-state sheriff, and multi-law-firm defense representation. The client exhibited a pattern of frequent missed calls (20+ over 7 months) with periodic attorney return calls.
+- In a slip-and-fall case at a national restaurant chain (DOI 5/2025), the client sustained injuries requiring surgery (orthopedic) with 13+ medical providers across two states (OH and KY). The BI claim was made directly against the restaurant chain's self-insured program — not through a traditional insurance carrier. The client was actively engaged, sending receipts and medical documentation via email. The firm noted the possibility of video evidence at the incident location, confirming the importance of early preservation requests for commercial PL cases.
+- In another slip-and-fall case (DOI 4/2025), the client had a significant pre-existing condition (prior ankle fracture with surgery in 2024) affecting the same body part. The BI claim was filed with a mutual insurance carrier. A health insurance subrogation lien was identified early. Records collection was complicated by difficulty obtaining orthopedic specialist records despite multiple fax requests — confirming the records bottleneck pattern in PL cases.
 
 ## Agent Guidance

Two of the settled BI claims were resolved separately per plaintiff, confirming that settlement amounts are independent even in joint litigation.

{"content": "    50|- The firm must quickly re-engage and catch up on delayed tasks
    51|
    52|## Evidence
    53|
    54|- In an auto accident case (DOI 3/2024), the client was unreachable from day one of file setup. Multiple calls were made with the note "No answer, vm is full." Text messages were sent as fallback. The client eventually responded via text ("I will call you later, thanks") and then called back from a different number. The voicemail transcription showed they had also been trying to reach the firm: "We have missed each other for the last day or 2." Despite repeated outreach attempts over weeks, the client remained difficult to reach throughout the case. By 2/2025, the note still read "Still unreachable." The client's phone number changed at least once during the case.
    55|- In the same case, even when the firm was trying to close/decline the case, a staff member noted they needed to speak with the client about the case update — and had to use a different phone number than the one on file.
    56|- In a PL case (DOI 9/2023) in active litigation, the client called the attorney repeatedly (20+ missed calls over 7 months), but the calls went unreturned for extended periods. The attorney then made periodic outbound calls. This is a reverse unreachability pattern — the firm is unreachable to the client rather than vice versa, likely due to attorney bandwidth in a litigation-heavy caseload.
    57|- In an elderly MVA case (DOI 1/2022) in litigation, the client's voicemail was explicitly noted as "full" when the firm attempted to call with case updates. The client called back repeatedly but calls were not answered. This voicemail-full pattern prevents both parties from leaving messages, creating a complete communication breakdown.
    58|- In an auto accident case (DOI 4/2023, KY) in active litigation, the firm attempted to call the client multiple times but the phone "just rings and then gives busy signal." The firm logged multiple attempts: "attempted to contact client, just keeps ringing" and "attempted to call client, just rings and gives busy signal." The client was eventually reached for a substantive conversation about the case status and defense counsel's settlement proposal. This is a distinct signal type — not voicemail full, not disconnected, but an indefinite ring/busy pattern suggesting a phone line issue or call blocking.
    59|- In an auto accident case (DOI 1/2024, KY) during a scheduled Zoom deposition, the firm had to text the client urgently: "Dana you good? We lost you" and "They were having some technical problems. Go ahead and log back on." The client was also texted earlier: "Just a reminder, the deposition starts in about an hour" and "if you use zoom before are you good with using that?" This shows a new unreachability pattern: technology-related unreachability during critical legal proceedings (depositions), where the client is willing but technically unable to participate, requiring real-time troubleshooting via text.
    60|- In a trucking MVA case (DOI 2/2024), the client's phone number was "not in service" when the firm tried to reach her during intake. A text was sent and a second number was tried. The client was eventually reached via an alternate number. This was a pattern of phone service disruption rather than willful non-response.
    61|- In an MVA case (DOI 1/2023), the client called the firm repeatedly but no one answered — calls were logged as "missed" to multiple staff members. The client also texted: "Is your office open today?" The firm's unreachability to the client (many missed inbound calls over months) is the reverse pattern, creating friction and frustration.
    62|- In an MVA case (DOI 1/2023), the client was initially unreachable — "call went to VM - left msg." The client then used an alternate phone number for subsequent contact. New phone numbers should always be captured immediately.
    63|- In a treatment-phase MVA case (DOI 8/2024), the client preferred text over phone calls. When staff called, the client was unavailable ("on an errand"). Text became the primary channel. Confirming preferred communication channel at intake prevents unnecessary phone-tag loops.
    64|
    65|## Agent Guidance
    66|
    67|1. If the first call goes to voicemail and VM is full, immediately send a text message
    68|2. If no response to text, send an email the same day
    69|3. Set a 2-week follow-up task automatically on every case where the client has not responded
    70|4. Log EVERY contact attempt (call, text, email) with outcome (answered, VM full, no answer, etc.)
    71|5. After 3 failed contact attempts, escalate to the attorney with a note about unreachability
    72|6. Always ask the client for a secondary contact number and an emergency contact
    73|7. When the client does respond, immediately gather all needed information in that same conversation — you may not get another chance soon
    74|8. Record the client's updated phone number in the case system immediately when it changes
    75|9. Consider whether unreachability is a pattern that makes the case unworkable — flag for attorney review
    76|
    77|## Pitfalls
    78|
    79|- Only trying to call and not using text/email as fallback
    80|- Not logging failed contact attempts (creates the appearance of no effort)
    81|- Waiting weeks between contact attempts instead of escalating channels
    82|- Not asking for a secondary contact number at intake
    83|- Not updating the phone number in the system when the client calls from a new number
    84|- Assuming unreachability means the client is uninterested — they may be dealing with medical/life issues
    85|
    86|## Related
    87|
    88|- [[client-frustration-escalation]] — opposite pattern where the client is trying to reach the firm
    89|- [[client-expectations-management]] — unreachable clients may still have expectations about case progress
    90|- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — unreachability most critically impacts intake tasks
    91|- [[case-decline-and-closing-workflow]] — persistent unreachability may contribute to case decline decisions
    92|", "total_lines": 91, "file_size": 8443, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

Confirming preferred communication channel at intake prevents unnecessary phone-tag loops.", "new_string": "- In a treatment-phase MVA case (DOI 8/2024), the client preferred text over phone calls. When staff called, the client was unavailable ("on an errand"). Text became the primary channel. Confirming preferred communication channel at intake prevents unnecessary phone-tag loops.
- In a demand-phase case (DOI 12/2025), the client's voicemail was full when the firm tried to call with a case update. The paralegal texted instead: "your voicemail is full so I couldn't leave a message." Client had called multiple times in the days prior (multiple missed calls logged) but the firm couldn't return calls. This is the classic bidirectional unreachability pattern — both sides trying to reach each other but missing.
- In a file-setup MVA case (DOI 8/2025), the client called multiple times (missed calls logged weeks apart) but received no callbacks. The case was in demand preparation but the client had no updates. The paralegal was uncertain what to tell the client — a pattern where the firm's inability to provide substantive updates compounds the communication gap.
- In an MVA case (DOI 9/2025) with disputed liability, both insurance carriers were denying fault. The paralegal escalated to the attorney multiple times ("Not sure what to tell the guy. Any ideas?") but received no response documented in the logs. The client's inability to get a substantive update — combined with the firm's uncertainty about the case strategy — represents a new pattern: strategy-driven unreachability where the firm avoids contact because it lacks a clear answer."}

Now let me create one new concept article for the one genuinely new pattern I identified — the "disputed liability stalemate" pattern seen in the Cory Stephenson case, where both carriers deny fault and the firm is stuck. Actually, looking more carefully, this is already covered by `coverage-denial-and-litigation` and `disputed-liability-forces-litigation`. The Stephenson case is a confirming case for the disputed-liability pattern.

The most notable new patterns that aren't well covered are:
1. **BI settlement triggering UIM claim** (Cynthia Gibson case - BI settled at limits, COOTS letter sent, UIM opened)
2. **Self-insured/restaurant entity BI claims** (Davis Robinson - Texas Roadhouse self-insured)
3. **Pre-existing condition complicating PL claims** (Tina Smith - prior ankle surgery)

These are confirmations of existing patterns rather than entirely new concepts. The BI-to-UIM transition is already in `uim-claim-management`. Let me add evidence to key articles and then write the log entry.

Let me add evidence to the key articles that benefit from this batch:

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Underinsured Motorist (UIM) Claim Management
     3|slug: uim-claim-management
     4|phase: [phase_1_file_setup, phase_4_negotiation, phase_7_litigation]
     5|category: financial
     6|tags: [uim, underinsured-motorist, insurance, allstate, parallel-claim, consent-to-settle]
     7|confidence: medium
     8|evidence_count: 8
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - pip-claims-management
    13|  - multi-carrier-negotiation
    14|  - litigation-process-patterns
    15|  - statute-of-limitations-monitoring
    16|---
    17|
    18|# Underinsured Motorist (UIM) Claim Management
    19|
    20|> When the at-fault driver's BI policy limits are insufficient to cover the plaintiff's damages, the plaintiff may pursue an Underinsured Motorist (UIM) claim against their own auto insurance carrier. UIM claims run as a parallel track to the BI claim and require distinct procedural steps including consent-to-settle notifications, separate complaint filing, and independent adjuster management — often with the same carrier that provides the client's PIP coverage.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|UIM claim management follows a parallel-track structure alongside the BI claim:
    25|
    26|### Setup
    27|1. **Identify UIM Coverage**: During file setup, check whether the client's own auto policy includes UIM coverage. If the at-fault driver's BI limits are low relative to the client's injuries, UIM becomes relevant.
    28|2. **Separate Claim Setup**: The UIM claim requires its own claim number and adjuster, even when the UIM carrier is the same company providing PIP.
    29|3. **Consent to Settle**: Before accepting the BI settlement, the UIM carrier must be notified and given an opportunity to consent (or subrogate). This is a critical procedural requirement.
    30|
    31|### Parallel Management
    32|- The UIM adjuster is separate from the PIP adjuster at the same carrier
    33|- The UIM claim number may be the same as or different from the PIP claim number
    34|- The UIM complaint is filed in the appropriate court (may be different jurisdiction than BI)
    35|- UIM litigation follows standard discovery and deposition procedures
    36|- The UIM carrier files an answer to the complaint and may retain defense counsel
    37|
    38|### Litigation
    39|- UIM complaints are filed against the client's own insurer — an adversarial posture against the client's own carrier
    40|- A courtesy copy of the complaint should be sent to the UIM adjuster
    41|- Discovery includes the plaintiff's first set of interrogatories/production requests to the UIM carrier
    42|- UIM litigation can proceed independently from BI settlement
    43|
    44|## Evidence
    45|
    46|- In Case G (auto accident, DOI 7/30/2023), the client's extensive injuries (including $45,830+ hospital bills, physical therapy, chiropractic care, and surgery) exceeded the at-fault driver's BI policy limits (State Farm, claim #1754K417G). The client had UIM coverage through Allstate Insurance (claim #723447272). The UIM claim required separate management:
    47|  - PIP adjuster (Margaret Sharp) handled PIP claim through Allstate
    48|  - UIM adjuster (Nick Burchette) handled the UIM claim separately
    49|  - A UIM complaint was filed in Fayette Circuit Court (24-CI-03580, "McCay v. Allstate Property and Casualty Insurance")
    50|  - Allstate filed an answer to the complaint
    51|  - A consent-to-settle form was obtained from Allstate before the BI claim could proceed
    52|  - Discovery commenced with plaintiff's first set of discovery to Allstate
    53|  - The case involved $319.82 in court filing fees, plus expenses for EvenUP ($500) and Digital Owl ($495) for case evaluation
    54|  - The client also had a Humana health insurance lien ($11,809) and pre-settlement funding ($31,200 from Legal Funding Partners)
    55|  - Defense counsel (Jennifer Ann Peterson) was retained by Allstate
    56|  - A separate "waiving subrogation" document was filed
    57|- In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2023, KY), the client's injuries included treatment at 8 providers (ER, chiropractic at multiple locations, radiology). The at-fault driver's BI coverage (through a state mutual insurer) was insufficient, triggering a UM claim through the client's own national carrier. A separate UM claim number was assigned and a distinct adjuster handled it. PIP was also through the same national carrier with yet another adjuster (the PIP team). The case entered litigation with all three insurance tracks active. This confirms the three-adjuster pattern at a single carrier: BI adjuster at the at-fault party's carrier, PIP team adjuster at the client's carrier, and UM adjuster at the client's carrier — each requiring separate tracking.
    58|- In an auto accident case (DOI 9/2021, KY), the case involved a UIM claim alongside the BI claim. The UIM carrier (a national insurance company) was the same as the PIP carrier. The UIM claim had its own claim number (same as PIP) but required a dedicated adjuster. Multiple adjuster reassignments occurred on the UIM claim. The case entered litigation after negotiation produced inadequate offers. This confirms that UIM adjuster reassignment follows the same pattern as BI adjuster reassignment, with the "fresh eyes" effect potentially unlocking movement.
    59|- In an MVA case (DOI 1/2023, KY), the at-fault driver carried Root Auto Insurance (BI), while the client's own Allstate policy provided both PIP and UM coverage. The UM claim shared the same claim number as PIP but had a different adjuster. The UM claim was designated "demand_pending" while the BI was "no_demand" — the primary recovery path was against the client's own UM carrier because the at-fault driver's BI coverage was insufficient. The case entered litigation (Jefferson 24-CI-004424) as a UM lawsuit against Allstate, with defense counsel retained by Allstate. The client also had a child support enforcement lien ($46K+), creating an unusual lien composition alongside the UM claim. Correspondence between plaintiff's counsel and defense counsel regarding scheduling/discovery confirms that UM litigation follows standard civil litigation procedures.
    60|- In a multi-carrier MVA case (DOI 1/2023, KY), the client had UIM coverage through Progressive (same carrier as PIP and BI). The BI claim against KFB was settled, while the UIM claim against Progressive went to litigation. The UIM adjuster was distinct from both the PIP adjuster and the BI adjuster — confirming the three-adjuster-at-one-carrier pattern. The case also had Medicare and health insurance liens, showing how UIM cases layer complexity on top of already complex lien management.
    61|- In Case Q (auto accident, VA veteran, DOI 5/2023), a three-carrier structure was observed: BI (Kentucky Farm Bureau), PIP (Everett Cash Mutual Insurance via a third-party administrator), and UIM (Erie Insurance Group). The UIM claim was managed by a dedicated adjuster at Erie. While the BI claim was in active litigation (Morgan County Circuit Court 24-CI-00065), the UIM claim remained on a parallel track with separate correspondence to the UIM adjuster. The case demonstrates that UIM can coexist with VA liens — the firm had to manage BI litigation, UIM parallel negotiation, PIP benefit tracking, AND a $86K+ VA lien simultaneously. The attorney was working on "distribution of funds" as of early 2025, indicating the BI component may have settled while UIM remained open. This confirms the pattern where UIM claims outlast BI resolution.
    62|
    63|## Agent Guidance
    64|
    65|1. At intake, identify the client's own auto insurance UIM coverage limits
    66|2. Compare the at-fault driver's BI limits to estimated damages — if damages likely exceed BI limits, flag for UIM
    67|3. Set up the UIM claim as a separate insurance claim record, even if it's the same carrier as PIP
    68|4. Track the UIM adjuster separately from the PIP adjuster
    69|5. Before accepting any BI settlement offer, obtain consent-to-settle from the UIM carrier
    70|6. Send a courtesy copy of the UIM complaint to the UIM adjuster when filed
    71|7. Track UIM litigation deadlines independently from BI claim deadlines
    72|8. When evaluating the total case value, consider BI limits + UIM limits as the recovery ceiling
    73|9. Budget for additional expenses: UIM litigation may require case evaluation tools (EvenUP, Digital Owl) and additional records requests
    74|
    75|## Pitfalls
    76|
    77|- Forgetting to check for UIM coverage during intake
    78|- Not obtaining consent-to-settle from the UIM carrier before accepting BI settlement (can waive UIM rights)
    79|- Confusing the PIP adjuster with the UIM adjuster at the same carrier
    80|- Not filing the UIM complaint timely (UIM has its own SOL considerations)
    81|", "total_lines": 89, "file_size": 8886, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=81 to continue reading (showing 1-80 of 89 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

While the BI claim was in active litigation (Morgan County Circuit Court 24-CI-00065), the UIM claim remained on a parallel track with separate correspondence to the UIM adjuster. The case demonstrates that UIM can coexist with VA liens — the firm had to manage BI litigation, UIM parallel negotiation, PIP benefit tracking, AND a $86K+ VA lien simultaneously. The attorney was working on "distribution of funds" as of early 2025, indicating the BI component may have settled while UIM remained open. This confirms the pattern where UIM claims outlast BI resolution.
- In an MVA case (DOI 7/2025), the BI carrier tendered its policy limits ($25K). After settling the BI claim, a COOTS (consent-to-settle) letter was sent, and a UM claim was opened with the client's own carrier (same carrier as PIP). The UM adjuster was assigned separately from the PIP adjuster. The firm began corresponding with the UM adjuster about medical records to support the UM claim. This confirms the standard BI-exhaustion-to-UIM-transition workflow: BI settles at limits → COOTS letter → UM claim opened → separate adjuster engagement → records provided to UM adjuster.
- In an MVA case (DOI 10/2023), the companion case involved a BI claim and a UIM claim against the same national carrier. The firm contacted the carrier to confirm no arbitration had been initiated. The case was complicated by disputed liability — both drivers blamed each other. The UIM claim remained in limbo while the liability dispute was unresolved, confirming that disputed liability can stall UIM claims just as it stalls BI claims.
- In an MVA case (DOI 6/2025), the case involved two BI carriers for two at-fault drivers, plus the client's own PIP carrier. While not a traditional UIM case, the multi-carrier structure demonstrates the same parallel-track management patterns. The firm tracked separate claim numbers and adjusters for each carrier independently."}

Now, let me update the `demand-preparation-workflow` with evidence from these cases:

{"content": "    47|## Evidence
    48|
    49|- In an auto accident case (DOI 2/2023), the client confirmed treatment was complete during a phone call on 3/11/2024 ("done treating with all medical providers listed on file. No more hospital visits."). Phase was changed to "Demand in Progress" that same day. Records review revealed a referral to White House Clinics and King's Eye Care not previously on file — both had to be added and records requested. Lien request was sent to The Rawlings Company (WellCare) during this phase. The BI adjuster (Clearcover) proactively called to follow up on claim status.
    50|- In the same case, records from providers were separated for relatedness (unrelated visits excluded) before demand preparation.
    51|- The demand was eventually sent, but the case then moved to "Settlement" and then back to "Negotiation" when the client refused the offer, indicating the demand-to-settlement pipeline is not always linear.
    52|- In Case M (MVA, DOI 8/2023), the demand preparation used EvenUP (a third-party AI demand tool) to generate the initial demand package. The attorney saved the EvenUP output to the file, then directed staff to edit it and send to both BI carriers. The demand was sent to both carriers on the same day (1/10/2024), with the case simultaneously transitioning from "Demand in Progress" to "Negotiation." The adjuster acknowledged receipt and promised evaluation "by the end of this week." The next day, policy limits (25/50) were tendered. This demonstrates the fastest observed demand-to-offer turnaround: 1 day from demand sent to limits tendered. EvenUP integration into demand preparation is a significant workflow accelerator.
    53|- In a treatment-phase MVA case (DOI 8/2024), the attorney directed: "Please see records and bills that we are missing. She's probably done treating at this point, and we can send the demand." Records were then requested from a dental school. The pattern of attorney-initiated demand readiness check followed by staff records gap-fill is consistent across cases.
    54|- In a treatment-phase MVA case (DOI 5/2024), the firm noted "spoke with client, he's sending over some bills he received. Need to review to make sure we have everything and move forward with demand." Follow-up requests for outstanding records/bills were sent. A PIP ledger update was requested. The SOL was 90 days away, creating urgency. The demand preparation was gated on receiving records from 3+ providers.
    55|- In a treatment-phase MVA case (DOI 5/2024), the attorney noted at intake: "Will need 5 years of priors from all providers other than EMS." This expanded the records-collection scope well beyond accident-related treatment, requiring historical medical records that take longer to obtain and increase demand preparation timeline.
    56|- In a trucking MVA case (DOI 2/2024), outstanding medical bills from a treatment provider were discussed in correspondence between the firm and the provider's billing office. The case was in litigation, but records completeness was still being addressed — demonstrating that demand preparation gaps can persist even after a case enters litigation.
    57|- In 6 cases from this batch (DOIs mid-to-late 2025, KY), the standard demand preparation workflow was observed: treatment confirmed complete, records/bills requested from all providers, paralegal followed up with providers for outstanding items, then demand sent to BI adjuster with records/bills attached. In one case, the firm sent a narrative report as supplemental support and demanded "policy limits." In another, a second round of records was needed from a provider who had done additional treatment after the initial demand materials were assembled. The pattern of "sent demands on both but [client] went back for some additional treatment with [provider] which she just completed. Sent request over to [provider] this morning. Should be quick to get wrapped up" confirms that late treatment can delay demand even after an initial package is prepared.
    58|- In cases with companion plaintiffs, the demand for both clients was sometimes sent to the same adjuster simultaneously — "I've attached the records/billing for [companion] as well. Please consider this our demand for policy limits." This batch-demand approach for companions sharing the same BI carrier is efficient but requires separate tracking.
    59|- The pattern of PIP ledger requests before demand finalization was confirmed across 6+ cases: firms routinely request PIP ledgers from carriers to understand what has been paid and what remains before calculating the demand amount. "Please forward a PIP ledger for our client" is a standard pre-demand task.
    60|- In cases with client at fault (1 case) and no BI/UM coverage (1 case), demand was never prepared — the case was declined before reaching that phase. This confirms that demand preparation requires viable BI or UM recovery path.
    61|
    62|## Agent Guidance
    63|
    64|1. When a client reports treatment is complete, confirm via phone and document the call
    65|2. Trigger the phase change to "Demand in Progress" and log who authorized it
    66|3. Immediately create tasks for any outstanding records/bills requests
    67|4. When records arrive, review them for references to other providers — add any new providers found
    68|5. Separate records by relatedness to the accident
    69|6. Send final health insurance lien requests (to Rawlings, Optum, etc.) to get lien amounts
    70|7. Compile all records, bills, and lien information for the attorney's demand preparation
    71|8. When an adjuster calls proactively, document the contact and provide a general update
    72|9. After demand is sent, create a 30-day follow-up task for adjuster response
    73|
    74|## Pitfalls
    75|
    76|- Moving to demand before all providers are identified (records review may reveal new ones)
    77|- Not separating related vs. unrelated treatment in the medical records
    78|- Forgetting to request health insurance lien amounts before the demand
    79|- Not documenting the client's confirmation that treatment is complete
    80|- Failing to follow up with the adjuster after demand is sent
    81|- Assuming the demand-to-settlement path is linear (client may reject the offer)
    82|
    83|## Related
    84|
    85|- [[medical-records-collection]] — records must be complete before demand
    86|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — demand is a formal phase change with prerequisites
    87|", "total_lines": 88, "file_size": 8830, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=87 to continue reading (showing 47-86 of 88 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

title: Disputed Liability Stalls Demand Phase

# Disputed Liability Stalls Demand Phase

> When both insurance carriers deny their insured's fault in an accident, the standard demand workflow stalls. The firm cannot send a demand because neither carrier will accept liability. The paralegal is left without a clear path forward and may avoid client communication because there is no substantive update to provide. This creates a cascading effect: the case sits in demand phase, the client becomes frustrated, and the paralegal repeatedly escalates to the attorney without resolution.

## Pattern

The disputed-liability demand stalemate follows a predictable sequence:

1. **Demand phase entry**: Treatment is complete and records are collected
2. **Carrier denial**: The BI carrier denies fault, claiming their insured had right-of-way or control of the intersection
3. **No arbitration**: The carriers have not initiated inter-carrier arbitration to resolve the liability dispute
4. **Paralegal uncertainty**: The paralegal does not know how to advise the client and escalates to the attorney ("Not sure what to tell the guy. Any ideas?")
5. **Attorney non-response or delay**: The attorney may not immediately respond, leaving the case in limbo
6. **Client frustration**: The client calls for updates but the firm has nothing to report
7. **Strategy-driven avoidance**: The firm may avoid proactive client contact because there is no good news to deliver

The resolution typically requires an attorney decision: file suit to force the liability issue, or attempt alternative dispute resolution.

## Evidence

- In an MVA case (DOI 9/2025), both BI carriers denied fault. The paralegal contacted one carrier and confirmed "no arbitration has been initiated." The paralegal then emailed the attorney: "Not sure what to do here, both sides blaming the other and not accepting any liability. No arbitration has been done etc." The attorney's response was not documented. Two weeks later, the paralegal escalated again: "State Farm still denying liability claiming their insured had control of the intersection. Stating point of impact confirms this." The paralegal then asked the attorney again: "Not sure what to tell the guy. Any ideas?" — indicating the case had been in this stalemate for weeks with no resolution strategy communicated to the paralegal.
- In previously documented cases, disputed liability at 25% acceptance forced immediate litigation. The key distinction in this new pattern is that the case has NOT yet moved to litigation — the stalemate is occurring during the demand phase, before any complaint is filed.

## Agent Guidance

1. When both carriers deny liability, immediately escalate to the attorney with a clear summary of each carrier's position
2. Do not let the case sit in limbo — request an attorney decision within 1 week: file suit, attempt mediation, or pursue inter-carrier arbitration
3. Communicate honestly with the client: "Both insurance companies are disputing who caused the accident. The attorney is evaluating our options, which may include filing a lawsuit."
4. Document the carrier's specific liability position (e.g., "claiming their insured had control of the intersection")
5. Check whether arbitration has been initiated between carriers — if not, ask the attorney whether to request it
6. Track SOL carefully — disputed liability cases can sit in this stalemate until SOL pressure forces action

## Pitfalls

- Allowing the case to sit in demand phase for months without attorney direction
- Avoiding client contact because there is no update — this compounds frustration
- Not documenting the specific liability positions of each carrier
- Assuming the carriers will resolve the dispute themselves without firm intervention

## Related

- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — demand cannot proceed when liability is disputed
- [[disputed-liability-forces-litigation]] — the eventual resolution is typically filing suit
- [[client-frustration-escalation]] — clients in stalled cases escalate through the frustration stages
- [[unreachable-client-patterns]] — strategy-driven avoidance is a form of firm-side unreachability
"}

Not sure what to tell her"), client requesting no blocked/unknown calls
- Case AD (auto accident, litigation, DOI 7/2024): 135 activity logs — KY bus accident, companion case (see Case AF), CAAL Worldwide + Unknown Driver defendants, PIP-only (National Indemnity), complaint filed Jefferson 25-CI-000133, Key Benefit Administrators lien ($358.51), SOL tracking (180 days), defense counsel correspondence

- Case AJ (slip/fall, demand, DOI 4/2025): 92 activity logs — KY slip-and-fall, pre-existing ankle condition (prior fracture + surgery 2024), West Bend Mutual BI, Carelon subrogation lien ($2,300.36), difficulty obtaining Norton ortho records despite multiple fax requests
- Case AK (auto accident, demand, DOI 9/2025): 91 activity logs — KY MVA, disputed liability (both carriers deny fault), State Farm BI denying liability, Elco PIP, no arbitration initiated, paralegal escalated to attorney multiple times ("Not sure what to tell the guy"), strategy-driven communication avoidance
- Case AL (auto accident, treatment, DOI 6/2025): 89 activity logs — KY MVA (NKY area), multi-carrier (National General BI + Progressive BI), orthopedic surgery with intraoperative discovery of torn rotator cuff, PIP via State Farm, client actively communicating via email/text

**Connection articles updated:**

### Confidence Upgrades (3 articles)
- `lien-management-health-insurance`: medium -> high (18 cases)
- `multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases`: medium -> high (11 cases)
- `medical-records-collection`: remains high (24 cases)

### Key New Patterns Identified
- **BI-exhaustion-to-UIM transition is a standard workflow**: When BI settles at policy limits, the COOTS letter → UM claim opening → separate adjuster engagement workflow is now documented from end-to-end in one case. This should be a checklist item.
- **Third-party claims administrator (TPA) demands require adjuster escalation**: When the BI claim is managed by a TPA (CCMSI, Texas Roadhouse self-insured), the demand may need to be re-sent to a different adjuster within the TPA when the first doesn't respond.
- **Disputed liability creates demand-phase stalemates**: When both carriers deny fault and no arbitration has been initiated, the case sits in demand phase with the paralegal unable to proceed. This is a distinct pattern from disputed liability forcing litigation — here the case is stuck BEFORE litigation.
- **Strategy-driven communication avoidance is a new unreachability pattern**: When the firm has no substantive update or strategy for a case, paralegals may avoid calling clients. This manifests as "Not sure what to tell her" and "Not sure what to tell the guy. Any ideas?" — distinct from the client being unreachable.
- **Pre-existing conditions in PL cases add complexity**: A slip-and-fall where the client had prior surgery on the same body part creates a defense argument about pre-existing condition aggravation.
- **Restaurant self-insured BI programs are distinct from traditional insurance**: Claims against restaurant chains may go through the restaurant's own self-insured program rather than a traditional carrier, changing adjuster dynamics.
- **PIP ledger-to-provider coordination is a pre-demand step**: Providers need to know what PIP has paid so they can adjust billing before the demand amount is calculated. This step ("Ledger attached. 7k paid to [provider]. Please resubmit billing") was confirmed across multiple cases.
- **Intraoperative discovery of additional injuries extends treatment**: Surgery may reveal injuries not previously diagnosed (torn rotator cuff discovered during shoulder surgery), extending the treatment timeline and delaying demand.
- **Client preference for no blocked/unknown calls**: At least one client explicitly told the firm not to call from blocked numbers — confirming that client communication preferences should be captured and honored at intake.

### Gaps Partially Addressed
- **BI-to-UIM transition workflow**: Now documented end-to-end from one case
- **TPA demand process**: First documented demand sent to a TPA
- **Disputed liability stalemate**: First documented demand-phase stalemate (vs previously only litigation-trigger)
- **Pre-existing condition PL cases**: First documented case with significant pre-existing condition

### Remaining Gaps
- BI-to-UIM settlement outcomes: UM claim opened but no settlement data yet
- TPA negotiation tactics: Only demand sent, no offer/counter data
- Disputed liability resolution without litigation: No example of carrier arbitration resolving fault dispute
- Restaurant PL case values: No settlement data for restaurant slip-and-fall cases
- Intraoperative injury discovery impact on case value: No settlement data to understand how additional injuries affect valuation
- Multi-PIP-carrier resolution: Uber case has 3 PIP carriers but no resolution data"}

+
+**Connection articles updated:**
+1. `disputed-liability-forces-litigation` — 4->5, confidence: medium (added demand-phase stalemate variant)
+2. `records-completion-gates-demand` — 13->14, confidence: medium (added ortho records blocking demand)
+3. `client-frustration-drives-premature-settlement` — 12->14, confidence: medium (added paralegal-uncertainty-driven avoidance)
+
+### Confidence Upgrades (3 articles)
+- `lien-management-health-insurance`: medium -> high (18 cases)
+- `multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases`: medium -> high (11 cases)
+- `medical-records-collection`: remains high (24 cases)
+
+### Key New Patterns Identified
+- **BI-exhaustion-to-UIM transition is a standard workflow**: When BI settles at policy limits, the COOTS letter → UM claim opening → separate adjuster engagement workflow is now documented from end-to-end in one case. This should be a checklist item.
+- **Third-party claims administrator (TPA) demands require adjuster escalation**: When the BI claim is managed by a TPA (CCMSI, Texas Roadhouse self-insured), the demand may need to be re-sent to a different adjuster within the TPA when the first doesn't respond.
+- **Disputed liability creates demand-phase stalemates**: When both carriers deny fault and no arbitration has been initiated, the case sits in demand phase with the paralegal unable to proceed. This is a distinct pattern from disputed liability forcing litigation — here the case is stuck BEFORE litigation.
+- **Strategy-driven communication avoidance is a new unreachability pattern**: When the firm has no substantive update or strategy for a case, paralegals may avoid calling clients. This manifests as "Not sure what to tell her" and "Not sure what to tell the guy. Any ideas?" — distinct from the client being unreachable.
+- **Pre-existing conditions in PL cases add complexity**: A slip-and-fall where the client had prior surgery on the same body part creates a defense argument about pre-existing condition aggravation.
+- **Restaurant self-insured BI programs are distinct from traditional insurance**: Claims against restaurant chains may go through the restaurant's own self-insured program rather than a traditional carrier, changing adjuster dynamics.
+- **PIP ledger-to-provider coordination is a pre-demand step**: Providers need to know what PIP has paid so they can adjust billing before the demand amount is calculated. This step ("Ledger attached. 7k paid to [provider]. Please resubmit billing") was confirmed across multiple cases.
+- **Intraoperative discovery of additional injuries extends treatment**: Surgery may reveal injuries not previously diagnosed (torn rotator cuff discovered during shoulder surgery), extending the treatment timeline and delaying demand.
+- **Client preference for no blocked/unknown calls**: At least one client explicitly told the firm not to call from blocked numbers — confirming that client communication preferences should be captured and honored at intake.
+
+### Gaps Partially Addressed
+- **BI-to-UIM transition workflow**: Now documented end-to-end from one case
+- **TPA demand process**: First documented demand sent to a TPA
+- **Disputed liability stalemate**: First documented demand-phase stalemate (vs previously only litigation-trigger)
+- **Pre-existing condition PL cases**: First documented case with significant pre-existing condition
+
+### Remaining Gaps
+- BI-to-UIM settlement outcomes: UM claim opened but no settlement data yet
+- TPA negotiation tactics: Only demand sent, no offer/counter data
+- Disputed liability resolution without litigation: No example of carrier arbitration resolving fault dispute
+- Restaurant PL case values: No settlement data for restaurant slip-and-fall cases
+- Intraoperative injury discovery impact on case value: No settlement data to understand how additional injuries affect valuation
+- Multi-PIP-carrier resolution: Uber case has 3 PIP carriers but no resolution data
